title: G.G. and Deanna Rose v. Salesforce.com, INC.
text: G.G (the victim) and Deanna Rose (the victim's mother) filed complaints against Salesforce.com, Inc (the defendant). After running away from home at age of thirteen, the victim had fallen prey to a sex trafficker organization that advertised women and minors for sexual purposes on the now-defunct platform Backpage.com. They contended that, despite being aware of Backpage's illicit actions, the defendant maintained a strong commercial relationship that enabled Backpage's growth, wilfully benefitting from its sex trafficking operations. According to the facts, Backpage and the defendant had a business relationship that included the sale of customized software and personalized support. The defendant was revealed to have met with Backpage, including its CEO, at least five times between November 2013 and April 2017 to learn about the company and examine its operating requirements. Therefore, it was argued that the defendant did not merely sell Backpage an off-the-shelf software package; rather, it sold a software created particularly for the platform and provided tailored assistance. Before the relationship with the defendant, Backpage had a gross income of $71 million in 2012. Backpage's gross revenue increased to around $346 million from January 2013 to May 2015, with nearly $340 million coming from adult advertising. Backpage's business expanded, and its profits increased, as did the extent of work covered by the defendant's contracts and its income from those contracts. The basis of the allegations was that the defendant should have known about Backpage’s involvement with sex trafficking, including minors, because in 2008, the US Attorneys General, along with law enforcement and Governors, openly recognized the platform as the major and most prominent sex trafficking and pimping website in the US; and in 2010, the National Association of Attorneys General widely referred to Backpage as a hub of human trafficking, particularly the trafficking of minors. The District Court first dismissed the complaint, claiming that Salesforce was protected by Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act and that the plaintiffs' allegations were insufficient. The Appeals Court, however, overturned this ruling, concluding that the plaintiffs' complaint constituted a legitimate claim under Section 1595 of the Act. It was determined that the victim was trafficked via Backpage, a well-known sex trafficking network whose actions had been criticized by authorities, and that the defendant's services facilitated and benefited from the platform's trafficking operations.
crime_types: Participation in an organized criminal group
